speaker: interviewer
text: We are just arriving in Lodz. You said that you were with your mother and your brother and sister and this other young girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe Lodz. From all descriptions, it certainly wasn't the pre-war city anymore. What do you remember about Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have never been in Lodz before in my life. I have never been any place, as a matter of fact, except Vilnius and the outskirts of Vilnius. So this is like going, really, overseas. I came to Lodz. It's a big city, dirty city. We were-- we were also-- there was a tremendous shortage in living areas. So through the Jewish organization, [NON-ENGLISH] through this organization, my mother got a apartment. And even that apartment, it reminded me a lot of ghetto. Because it-- of course, I-- there was not a comparison. We were free. But even that apartment was small, tiny, little apartment also, divided, divided to another family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on top of it all, it was a very religious couple, a hazzan with his wife. You know what a hazzan is? OK. With his wife. So this apartment was also divided with curtains. I don't know what it was about this curtain business in Europe. They divide it. And they-- they shared-- we shared the apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very difficult. My mother wanted right away for us somehow to teach us about Judaism. Because, you know, we really didn't know anything. So right away, she signed us up to Hebrew schools, myself and my sister. We could not-- there was no place where to study. There was no place where to bake. There was no place, absolutely, this tiny, little [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The street [NON-ENGLISH] and the number 42, I remember in Lodz. So we-- so-- and at the same time, there were a lot of, from the same organizations, Jews that were making places available for Jewish children that were orphans, so have orphans a place where they can study, a place where they can sleep and-- and be more-- more-- more geared for children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother consented for us to-- to be there. And it was good [INAUDIBLE] one of the very-- that we end up-- very religious children's home. It was a very nice place really to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mom was not far. She was on the same street, the number was 42. And we were on [NON-ENGLISH] 66. In the same place, there was a synagogue. There was a very orthodox kitchen. And Jews were gathering constantly. And all the news of the world you could find out over there. And also, you could change some dollars over there, too. They had like a-- like a stock market going there. It was very vibrant. And so we lived there for a little bit, went to school. I-- I'm sorry to say I wanted more education than what it was provided for me in the Hebrew school. I did not like the method of teaching. Because we had like a [NON-ENGLISH]. You're not to [? speak. ?] You're not supposed to ask questions. You just learn, you know, what they told you to learn. My sister didn't mind. But I wanted to go to regular school, to a regular high school. And so it was very funny thing, because from this orthodox place to send me to regular school, it was unheard of.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had-- I had so I had a chaperone on the bus, going with me to school, sitting in the hallway. For a little bit, not for very long. But in the beginning, so I wouldn't mix in too much with the other kids, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that wasn't going on for very long. And I did go to regular Polish high school. And that's how I learned very well Polish. I speak and read and write very fluently. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My sister didn't. My sister went to Hebrew school and loved it, didn't want to go any other place. She just accepted everything that was-- that was Jewish and that was-- didn't ask really many questions either, except my mother didn't ask her any questions either and was a very happy child, actually. She was a happy-go-lucky child and came from time to time with a new story about Israel and how one day, we are going to go there. I didn't know that my mother was plotting already to leave Poland at that time. I was making plans to finish high school. The nuns taught us very well. We didn't realize it. But when we came to school, I was really on a very high level in most of the subjects, except like political science. I didn't know what's that. I didn't-- History, I didn't know very much either, because they didn't talk about it. But the literature and the language and math and all this, I was really on a very good level there. So I didn't have any problems in school. And so very soon, I found out that I am very fascinated with the human body and all this and was beginning to think even, perhaps, to go to medical school and making plans, making plans, and already beginning to look to pay attention to a young man there. So I didn't know that my mom was planning already to leave-- to leave for Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she did?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you all go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we in-- so we came to Israel in 195-- 1952 I think. I think 195-- oh, god. I should remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Early '50s.

speaker: interviewee
text: Early '50s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was life in Israel for you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very difficult for me. In the beginning, it was very difficult. Because, again, we didn't-- they didn't integrated us right away into the society. We had to be in a camp. It's called [NON-ENGLISH]. People from all over the world with different cultures, different languages, different looking put into one place together. It was very overwhelming for me. We-- my sister spoke fluently Hebrew, so it wasn't difficult for her. I spoke a little bit, not much, because I preoccupied with my studies in high school. So and-- so I wasn't as fluent as she was. And she accepted, again, everything what's in there and just flourished and loved it and didn't pay attention to the fact that we were, again, in some kind of a camp. And-- but I did pay attention to all this. And I didn't like it. So it took-- it took quite a-- it took time for me to-- to get used to the life in Israel. After this, my mother found a place for us in a very beautiful, beautiful, village, in Tiv'on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tiv'on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And we rented an apartment. She brought a little bit of money with her from-- she brought dollars and African money, too. And so she had enough when we came to-- to rent an apartment, a very nice-- small, but very nice apartment in Tiv'on. And very quickly, we got integrated to the society there, to the village people. It's a lovely, lovely place. It's even more beautiful now. When I go there, I always-- when I go to Israel, I always go to see that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Israel grows on you, you know? You become part of it. You become part. You become-- you have a sense of belonging. You have a sense of roots.

speaker: interviewee
text: I live in the United States for a long time now, over 40 years. I love it here. I think this is a neat country, wonderful country. And I really don't want to live any other place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yet, when I go to Israel, it's like I came home. I-- I-- people didn't see me for a year or two, and from-- from across the street they shout, [NON-ENGLISH], shalom. [NON-ENGLISH]. What is it? How are you? Just like-- like it-- like I never left.

speaker: interviewee
text: There is a tremendous sense of belonging and acceptance. It's OK. You are OK with all your shortcomings, with all your blond looks and not this Aryan look. You know, you are fine. You are OK. You are one of us and that kind of a feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every year, sometimes twice a year, I go there. I need this-- I need this-- I need this for my soul to refurbish my soul, to make OK with the world. I go on Mount Carmel when my sister lived there. There are beautiful parks there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't do there when I go there anything special. I visit with my sister. And we go to-- to this-- on the very top of the mountain. And we sit there, and we reminisce. And sometimes we are very, very quiet. And we're being replenish. Like, like, like I don't know if you understand what I am saying. It's like my soul being filled. And it's OK with the world. And then when I come back, I feel good for another several months and-- and a year. And then I'm looking again forward to going there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, isn't that strange? I was born in Lithuania. My father and my father's fathers have been born there and lived there. The house that we lived, it came from my grandfather. Many generations lived there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't feel like that about Lithuania. I don't feel like that about my city or even the house. I do want to go there. I wanted to stand in front and look at it again. I wanted to make pilgrimage to the cemetery, because there are a lot of people of ours that are lived and buried there. But there is nothing for me. There is an emptiness over there and-- and-- and-- and-- and sorrow and nothing. And in Israel, I feel-- I feel this is mine. As little as it is with all the problems that it is, it's mine. I can go there anytime I want to. I can come and live here and love this country very much. And yet, I-- I-- I like to go there, too. And-- and that feeling that I get there, it's beyond-- beyond any kind of description.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in Israel-- funny, I lived in Israel a very short time, really. Because I met my husband there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph. And we were neighbors with his sister. His sister lives in Tiv'on Qiryat [INAUDIBLE]. She was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was just there on a visit?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was just there on a visit, yes. And-- and I marry-- I met him and married him there. We had a very orthodox wedding. [LAUGHS] And but then, I couldn't come right away to the United States, because of my background. They found out that I was in high school, in high school, a very communistic high school. There were no other high schools in-- in Poland at that time. But still--

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't enroll in the non-communist high school.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was none. The high school was a communistic organization. So I had to go through all sorts of cleansing, like, sort of here, through the United States government until they decided that it's OK that I'm not a dangerous person to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you married, and then you had to separate immediately after the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Then he came to the United States. He was a citizen. And I had to still wait for about two years until I came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your arrival in the United States was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wonderful. It was overwhelming. I came on a boat. And it was to New York. And I remember my husband took me out to a very elegant restaurant. And they gave me a steak. And it was rare, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I thought I'd just about died. [LAUGHS] I never saw that in my life. I sliced it, and there was this blood. And I thought, oh, my god, what's going on over here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was overwhelmed with New York. I heard so much about it. I used to love to watch the Westerns. And you know, I wanted to see a cowboy some place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find any in Canton?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came in Canton-- to Canton, it looked like a little village to me. Because after all, I was in Lodz. It's a big city, then in Tel Aviv. I was going to nursing school by then. And it was a very bubbling city, Tel Aviv. And then in New York, of course, New York was a very enchanting place. I-- I-- I didn't have enough. It was overwhelming, but I wanted to see everything. And but when I came to Canton, it was very quiet and very different from what I have known before. It took quite some time for me to used to. People were very nice to me. I met Rabbi Goren, who right away asked me if I would teach Hebrew. And I-- I told him, I want you to know-- you have to know my background. And if you know my background, I don't know if you want me to teach Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the Hebrew school dropout.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Family where?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you had children in Canton?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My children were born in Canton.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mark and Esther. Mark is for my father, named after my father. My father wasn't Mark, Moshe. But Moshe-- but who are you going to call Moshe over here, you know? So-- so-- and Esther is named after Joseph's mom. They-- Mark and Esther didn't know about my background at all. Mark found out not long ago, as a matter of fact, when he was doing some-- he asked me a few times. And I-- I didn't really want to sit down and tell him about everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know. I don't know. I cannot give you exact answers why. I think, because I wanted to protect them from all this. And what good? What good would it do for them to suffer and to know what their parents went through? We are here in a different country. And it's a wonderful country. With all its shortcomings, it's still better than anything else that I can imagine in the world. So let him grow tall and strong and happy and educated and do all the things that he wants to do, without my-- my telling about my miserable stolen-- my stolen childhood, my stolen teenage years, my stolen life, early life. Why-- why should I? So I didn't want to. And I didn't talk any place either. I very-- I'm very late bloomer.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- [INAUDIBLE] started to bug me about-- and my friend. I had a very good friend here, Dr. Stabholz, who wrote a book and called it He told me that it's not good to keep it like this, that it's very important for you to either to write or to talk about it and to teach other kids, you know, about it. Not kids only, people, you know, about what really had happened. And-- you know, because knowledge, there is a power in knowledge. And they can change-- you can change things. Things don't have to necessarily repeat itself. So-- so they convinced me. And my first speech was in Louisville School. The kids were in high school. This is the high school. And I told Mark that I'm going to talk there, and I feel very strange. I have never spoken before so many people, and especially kids. I-- I-- you know, but I did go. I did go. And he went with his camera. So that that's how he found out, actually, in details, things about me, at least. About his father he knew a little bit more. And that was the first time for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what you're doing with your days now. You still work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you-- yes, I do work. And I love my work, I love what I'm doing. I tell you, it is so gratifying. I can't even begin to express. I miss family. I-- I-- I-- I-- I for me, family is very important. The people that I take care of in Menorah Park, they all came from somewhere. Some of them are Holocaust survivors. Some of them are not Holocaust survivors. They came from other parts of Eastern Europe. But they came here not because they were tourists, not because they wanted to come here. Well, they probably, you know, America, it's always the dream of everybody. But because they were destitute where they were. There were tremendous prejudices and pogroms. And they were running from something. And mostly, mostly, because they were Jewish. They were-- they were prosecuted, not even in Holocaust, after the Holocaust, in Russia. These people went through tremendous hardships. And finally, they came-- they came to United States. The ones that were younger, they integrated into the society. And most of them are educated, and they're doing fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what about the older people? It's very hard to for them to change. It's very hard that the culture is so different. It's very hard. So they found themselves very often in Menorah Park. Also-- This is the Jewish Home for the Aged in Cleveland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Cleveland. And they-- and even being in Menorah Park, there are so many problems that they-- that they encounter, language, not understanding what's happening. And even the law of kosher food, they were not allowed to practice any religion in-- in Russia. They were- they were, you know-- so even the religion they don't quite understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I found myself constantly interpreting, reassuring. And-- and-- and many of them speak Yiddish like my mom used to. So-- so I think I'm taking care of all my moms and all dads. And-- and they waiting for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so-- so many times I wanted to quit already. And sometimes I even take off for two weeks or longer. And I begin to miss them. And I know that they miss me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're there as a nurse? We should establish that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a charge nurse--

speaker: interviewer
text: Charge nurse.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on a wellness unit. But truly, what I do as a nurse anybody can do. It's not what I do as a nurse. It's what I do-- what I do. I-- I-- I-- I-- I am there for them. I listen. And I know what I hear. And I know what I hear even when they don't speak. I know what they-- I know what they feel. I feel the same way. They are-- I know what makes them tick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so I very much believe in Menorah Park concept, that there is a place like this for Jewish people. Because where else would they go? You just tell me. Where would they go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they also have sense of-- after they get used to a little bit, they have a sense of belonging. And there is a rabbi. And there are celebrations of holidays. And they talk about their background. And they talk. I-- I-- I listen. Because I know what they talk. And when I put a music that I know that they are familiar with, because I heard this music when I was a little girl, you should see their faces. They smile, and they begin to hum and sing. Or, if I put Hebrew songs for people that-- the same thing. I-- I-- I feel very-- I love-- I love that God direct me-- directed me-- I think that's the only way I know how to explain-- into a place to do that kind of work. Listen, not everything is pleasant, you know. People grow older, and then they die. But they die from the old age. Nobody killed them. And nobody told them how long they should live, or if they are allowed to live. They die with their old age. And the ones that have grandchildren and children, they come there, surrounded by their grandchildren and children. And the ones that don't have anybody, I making sure that they are not alone. So, you know, it's-- it's very gratifying. So I don't know how long I'm going to work there. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't think they'll let you leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- they-- they awarded me. I don't think I deserved that award, because-- because they gave me an award in excellence in nursing I very proudly display in one of my rooms here. But it's not the nursing that gives me so much pleasure. It's the nurturing that I'm able to give, besides nursing. Even if I wouldn't be a nurse, I would be-- it would be very gratifying to me that I know that I see a smile where there was a fright before, that I know that this person will not sleep in a dark room. And I know why. And I know that another person likes to take a lot of stuff to his or her room with food. And I know why is that. And I know-- and I know that dogs, it's not a very good idea. I know that there is this animal interaction with the patients and stuff. But I know my people. And they don't really care very much. Or, they care very much for people. And this is not very good. So I was glad when they put the fish tank downstairs. And that, too, I thought that gefilte fish they liked better.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] Thank you. What picture are we looking at?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother. This is before I was born. This picture, she had already two children, two-- two older sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you have it?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a young woman. We had those pictures-- this and other pictures, we got it from our nanny, the [NON-ENGLISH] that I was mentioning before. Those are the only pictures that we really have, what was left. This picture is-- the war was going on already. And it was made for an ID very shortly before we-- we went to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is on the left, and who's on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother. This is my mother and I. I was so light that they-- that I remember they had to put dark cloth behind me. Otherwise, I was blending into the wall. This is my sister and I. And that was war going on. And that was-- this is already a ghetto, in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leah.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Barbara. That's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is Leah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, Leah on the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my sister Leah, the one that I was in the convent with. She lives in Israel now. This picture is made two years after the war, our first-- first day in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- that's I am. And it's also made two years after the war. And that's for school passport the picture, two year.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your sister are wearing the same outfit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: School uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my mother. And that's-- that's how she looked later. That's about a year and a half, two years before she died. She didn't look so good here. But her sweet smile is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are these?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my children. That's Esther and her husband and Matthew and little Evan. They all live in Columbus now. This is a recent picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And here is my oldest grandson, Aaron. He is 15 now. And that's me there. He's a very nice young man. He plays piano beautifully. I'm very proud of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to introduce my son Mark and my husband Joseph. And here I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just wanted to say a few words on camera. Mom and Dad, I'm most proud to be your son. I realize this was a difficult experience. And although I will never truly understand what you went through, I hope this process will facilitate the healing. It's a survival of the heart that impresses me the most, survival of the spirit. I do this for you, for my son Aaron, for my sister's children, Matthew and Evan Brody, so that they will-- so that they and future generations never forget what so few lived to tell. Thank you.
